speaker: interviewer
text: This is take 3. The date is January 16, 1997. I'm conducting an interview with Barbara Marlow. The location is London, England. Will you tell us what happened when you went back?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came back in the evening and we walked into that workshop. And this memory is of absolute stillness and yet the place being alive. There was no one there. There were all the sewing machines lined up. And needle halfway through a piece of fabric, chair overturned. It was an eerie feeling. And there was a kitchen where they were preparing a meal. There were half peeled potatoes. Everything was just all over the place. And there were no people, no sounds.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as we were standing there suddenly a trap door in the floor opened. And this old lady appeared, just poked her head through. And said, are they gone? I don't know how old she was because, you know, being young you regard as anybody over 50 as old. I don't know how old she was. But we just looked at her. Somebody said, are you alone? What are you doing here? And she said when they came she panicked and she run and hid in the cellar. And the poor woman just burst into tears and said, what am I doing here? They've taken my daughter, they've taken my grandson. I don't want to live. And that was an absolute nightmare seeing here. I think that brought it more to me what had happened than anything else. Of course, my grandmother was gone, my aunt was gone.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that same evening the telephones were still working in the ghetto. And they were not only within the ghetto but outside as well. We found out that in fact they were all taken to the Umschlagplatz. The hospital was a hospital which bordered onto the Umschlagplatz. And the director of the hospital Mr. [NON-ENGLISH] saw my grandmother from the window however in the crowd. And of course he knew her because she was on the various committees of friends of the hospitals and so on. And they got her out. They got her into the hospital. So my aunt was deported but my grandmother was saved for the moment. At that point, I think-- maybe before that-- but my mother had decided I had to be got out of the ghetto because it was becoming too dangerous for me as a child. And it must have been arranged before because it couldn't have been done in such short notice. I was to go out with the work column the following morning. So I was dressed up in a long skirt. Fortunately I was quite tall so I looked older than my 12 years. I had a scarf over my head. And I was padded out. I think I had about three changes of clothes on me. And we went to one of the gates where a work column was forming. And my mother had told me that she would go and see grandmother and try and arrange for her to leave the ghetto as well and then she would join me. But as we were standing in this column and we were just about to leave I suddenly panicked and said to my mother if you don't come with me I'm not going. Well, everybody around us started getting agitated in case the Germans spotted something going on. So they said, OK. Don't say anything. Just stand there. And we left together. I probably saved her life. In any case, we left the ghetto and at some point along the route of the column separated from the column and went into the doorway of an apartment block.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there guards with this column?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I remember. I don't think so. I think there were leaders but they would have been-- I don't whether they were Jewish or Aryan. But they would have been the ones who had been paid to allow us to get out. So we went into the gateway to change or take off some of the clothes to look normal. And that was the first time when somebody approached my mother and said they had seen us leave the column and would she contribute. I don't know what she paid. But that was-- we left there and then we went to my old nanny's flat which wasn't very far away. And she had agreed to look after us. But unfortunately being quite a simple woman, she was so thrilled to know that we were safe that she took me out shopping and in every shop she was telling everybody isn't it wonderful that her Barbara had escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she knew you were Jewish and the risk she was taking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But she wasn't bright enough. Again, to her it really didn't mean anything our being Jewish. It made no difference. She's had worked for other Jewish families. She was devoted to us. She was the one came to nurse me when I had scarlet fever. She was always there for me. So what can one say?

speaker: interviewer
text: Which part of Warsaw was this where she lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: She lived quite near what the part would became the little ghetto in [NON-ENGLISH]. And the shopping-- there was a big marketplace very near. So that's where we went. And she had offered to have my grandmother to stay. And my mother spoke to my grandmother on the telephone and she was trying to make arrangements for her to leave the ghetto. But it was slightly more complicated because my grandmother having been educated in Germany although she didn't speak Yiddish she spoke German. She did have an accent in Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you mean that her accent was a Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And she also had more Semitic features perhaps than my mother and myself. So that's was an added hazard. But my nurse was quite prepared to have her to stay. But by that time I think mother was getting wary.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name this nurse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maniunia, but I can't remember what her-- I can't remember what her surname was.

speaker: interviewer
text: But Maniunia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Maniunia, yes. I've got her photograph somewhere but I can't remember what her name was, surname. And we had, we moved on from there. All this time we were being looked after by this friend who had helped us with food in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you have with you? Just those clothes no doll or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just the clothes that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just what we--

speaker: interviewer
text: --those layers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. You couldn't take anything with you. But during that time and afterwards one got used to having nothing. I found it quite difficult after the war to start getting attached to things because things were something which you left behind. They didn't matter. You just didn't have anything. You didn't expect to have anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then we left Maniunia place. And I think we went to Mrs. Biedrzycka, or we went straight to stay in a boarding house in the country. I can't remember exactly. But at that point the problem was we had no papers. That had to be arranged. So in this place in the country we met a priest who was staying in the same boarding house. He was there with his cousin. And they took a great liking to me. So we talked a lot. And I loved reciting poetry. And one of my favorite poems of that time was called [POLISH] after the Jewish prayer. It was written by a Polish poet but it was an extremely poignant poem. Could you remember it now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of it, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you say some of it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well in Polish. [POLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's interesting because it goes on. It's quite a long poem. Could you translate some of what you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was just going to say that it says it was in a courtyard of a Jewish house on a summer's evening I hid there. Faith instead of uniting divides people. It was the wedding of Isaac with the secondhand [INAUDIBLE] daughter. And among the walls of the house moved waves of dark heads. And then it goes on about the ceremony itself and about the prayer, [HEBREW], for the father of the bride who had died in his youth. It's a wonderful poem. And one which spoke an awful lot to me. And especially this verse about faith separating people rather than uniting them.

speaker: interviewer
text: In any case, a day or two later the priest spoke to my mother and said he hopes she doesn't mind but are we in hiding? And are we Jewish and can he help in any way. And my mother told him that we were converts. That my father was in the Polish army. And yes, the great thing he could do for us would be to procure papers. The best papers you could have was a birth certificate of somebody of the right age who was dead and didn't have family. So he said that's something which he can arrange. And he got us birth certificates. The only problem was that the one he got for me was one of a country girl with a very peasant name which I found quite unacceptable. Although I knew I had to change identity, but this identity really was not something which pleased me. What was the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name was [? Mariana Anishka. ?] I hated this Christian name. I hated the surname. I just felt it wasn't me. It didn't fit my own image of myself. My mother did all right. She was called [? Paulina Karcheska. ?] And that was much better.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you weren't meant to be related?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. No. I mean there was no way in which we could stay together. That was too dangerous. We had lived in the center of Warsaw. There was an added complication that few years before when I must have been about six or seven. I had a series of [? polyphoto ?] pictures. 48 pictures on a sheet taken. And without my parents consent an advertising agency got hold of those pictures. And when we came back from holidays that summer, I was plastered all over the pharmacies of Warsaw advertising cod liver oil. One picture where I had turned my head away had a spoon of one make of cod liver oil, which I didn't like apparently. Another one where I was smelling a rose they substituted a spoon. And I was all smiles and delight. And that was a brand.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when those were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I must have been about six, seven. Well, it ended in the courts. Because in those days it wasn't really acceptable to have your child advertising. And it was done without my parents' consent. But my face was quite well known. Apparently I was walking around and saying, she looks just like me. But I do like my cod liver oil. [LAUGHS] So my face was fairly well know. They wouldn't have-- nobody would have known I was Jewish but even so it didn't do to be known. So that was a sort of the first help we got from this priest. And he was called Father Pavlina. He was working for a Catholic organization called [INAUDIBLE]. And he did a great deal of good work working with the underground and so on. Very sadly, he was arrested by the communists after the war and either murdered or died in prison. He also arranged for me to go to a convent school, a boarding school run by nuns outside Warsaw where I spent six months. The nuns did not know that I was Jewish. But there was another Jewish girl there who somebody identified as being Jewish and the nuns asked her to leave or asked her guardians to take her away. And she said that if she has to leave she will tell them that there is another Jewish girl. How she knew, I don't know. But she guessed. And that was the end of my boarding school. I was there from 14th of February 1943 until the summer. Or was it-- yeah, it must have been '43. I remember the date because when I arrived there there were Canadian nuns. It was the order of St. Felix. And they were celebrating St. Valentine's Day, the first time I've ever heard of St. Valentine's. So that identifies the date when I arrived there. I don't know when I left. After that I moved around. Also before I went to the convent I move around staying with various people having been brought up by a patriot father, I knew that there was an underground and a resistance movement. Mrs. [NON-ENGLISH] daughter was very active in the girl guides. And I was desperate to join. Everybody was a bit wary. I would be hiding on two counts. But I was allowed to join a unit. And that was a great boost to me. And one of my friends in the guides was Hania Nowodworska, whose father had been the president of the Lawyers' Association and as such was arrested by the Germans when they occupied Warsaw. He was then released but his health had suffered enormously and he died shortly afterwards. Her mother was a very active member and one of the leaders of an association that was looking at the political prisoners in Warsaw's jails and did wonderful work passing messages and ensuring that the prisoners knew what they were supposed to say, coordinating what was said by different people at great risk to themselves. At one stage I stayed with her. And somehow in conversation we were sitting together burning law books in a fireplace to keep warm. I let slip names of lawyers in the family, one of my grandfather's sister had married a leading Polish lawyer. He was Jewish but he was a member of the commission for the codification of Polish law after the formation of the Polish state. His son was a top criminal lawyer in the war, yes. In any case she knew then of my origin. She also knew that my father was in the Polish army. And subsequently she agreed to let a room to my mother who stayed there up to the time of the Warsaw rising. It wasn't done for money. When my mother had no more money to pay for her room, her rent, which was normal rent, she was told there was no problem. All accounts could be settle after the war. She was not only prepared to let my mother live there but she really kept her morale up by letting her go out with her youngest daughter who was only about four at the time. Taking all these risks. Also, as I said, she was a member of this political prisoners relief association, and they organized clandestine concerts to raise funds for the prisoners. And on one or two occasions when her daughter was selling programs at one of the concerts, she suggested I should go with them as well. My mother was very apprehensive. She said too many people in Warsaw know Barbara. And she said if she's with me she's all right. So I did which was a great morale booster. We were also visiting and being helped by a family whose sons were at school with my brother. It was Professor Leonard of the Academy of Fine Arts in Warsaw and his wife. They had two sons who were involved in the underground. In fact, the boys from my brother's secondary school formed one of the elite resistance units. And they treated me like a younger sister. I remember on my mother's birthday her being presented with a basket of vegetables. Not flowers, but a wonderful basket of vegetables from their [? allotment ?]. So we did have very, very good friends who looked out for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: While all this was happening and you were managing to move around a little bit in Warsaw, what were you aware of that had changed in the city outside the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was constant danger. Every time you went out, you didn't know whether you were going to get back. There were roundups in the streets. You could be-- if a German didn't like you, he could shoot you on the street. He could kick you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whether you were Jewish or Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was assumed at that point in the streets you were Polish. It made no difference. Trams in the city they had sections for Poles. You were hanging onto outside railings of the tram while the Germans inside were sitting very comfortably, thank you. You were not allowed to go to the cinema. The schools were limited to elementary schools. Other than that there were trade schools. Life was full of dangerous.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get any news from within the ghetto during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but I remember in 1943 at Easter there is a custom in Poland that in churches they have graves of Christ prepared with flowers and lights. And people visit graves in different churches. And I remember going to the cathedral which is in the old city of Warsaw and very close to the Jewish quarter. And I remember on that occasion the shooting and the fires because the ghetto uprising had started. So I was very well aware of that. But other than that, there was no one to ask and nobody knew anything. Nobody could tell you. All they could say was they are fighting in the ghetto but that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did you thing about where your grandmother had gone by then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I knew that she had been deported by then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have any inkling of what might have happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I mean there was my grandmother, there were two of my mother's sisters and their families. There was my grandmother's bother and his wife. I think at that time this wasn't something which frightened you. It was an everyday thing. You knew you could die whether you were Jewish or you were Polish. It just-- it was a fact of life. You were alive or you could be deported to Germany for labor. You could be killed. There was no stability, security. You just took each day as it came. You just had to adapt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Through your contacts did you have any news of your father and brother in this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Occasionally we had something Mrs. Biedrzycka via Switzerland. My father had a friend in Switzerland who used to transmit information because you could write to neutral country from England.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that through the Red Cross?

speaker: interviewee
text: That wasn't through the Red Cross. That was direct mail. And then there were the Red Cross cards. Or they were limited to I think twenty words or something like that. There were also Red Cross parcels which used to come to Mrs. [NON-ENGLISH] for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was in those parcels?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sardines. Sardines, chocolate, I can't remember anything else. But you know they were far in between. I can't even remember that there was close particular excitement. It was much more exciting having news of my father. My friends who were not aware that I was Jewish and I was hiding, they took it for granted that my father was in the Polish army therefore I was in hiding. Because several of them were in the same situation. My patrol leader her mother was in Auschwitz because she was assistant to one of the leaders of the resistance and was arrested with him. She's survived and today is a lady in her 90s living in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Helena Czarnocka. So you know there were all these things which happened. And the girls took it for granted that I'm in hiding, my father is in the army. So I was on the same terms. And that suited me because that's what I felt.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's a good place to finish.
